name: Unk’Tomi “Tomi” Blackshield (Earth-718’s Spider-Woman)
description:
Unk’Tomi “Tomi” Blackshield

Early Life: Roots in the Rez (Birth to Age 6)

Unk’Tomi Wiyaka Blackshield entered the world on a chilly September morning in 2006, born in a small clinic on the Pine Ridge Reservation in South Dakota. Her mother, Winona Blackshield, a 25-year-old Lakota beadworker and community health aide, labored alone for hours, supported only by a few midwives humming old songs to ease the pain. Winona had grown up in the shadow of generational trauma—her own parents survivors of boarding schools that stripped away language and traditions. Tomi’s father, Elias Crowe, a mixed Lakota-Cherokee mechanic with a restless spirit, had vanished months earlier, chasing work off-rez and leaving Winona to face motherhood solo. “He was like the wind,” Winona would later tell Tomi, “always moving, never staying.” Named Unk’Tomi after the Lakota trickster spirit Iktomi—the spider who weaves clever webs to outsmart foes—baby Tomi was seen as a symbol of resilience, a little weaver meant to mend what was broken.

Life on Pine Ridge was raw and unfiltered, much like Mary Brave Bird’s accounts of fatherless cabins without plumbing or power. Tomi’s first home was a weathered trailer shared with extended family, where winters bit hard and summers brought dust storms that choked the air. Money was scarce; Winona scraped by selling beaded jewelry at powwows and working odd jobs at the local clinic, treating everything from diabetes to despair. Tomi, a curious toddler with wide eyes and a mop of black hair, spent days toddling after cousins, climbing scrubby hills like a tiny explorer. She absorbed stories from elders around campfires—tales of Iktomi’s pranks, the Ghost Dance, and warriors like Crazy Horse. These weren’t just bedtime stories; they were survival tools, teaching her to be cunning in a world stacked against Natives.

By age 4, Tomi showed an early spark of that trickster energy. She’d “weave” elaborate games with string and sticks, pretending to trap imaginary monsters, foreshadowing her later abilities. But hardship hit early: At 5, Winona’s mother (Tomi’s grandma Lila) succumbed to untreated health issues, a common rez tragedy echoing the systemic neglect Brave Bird described. Lila’s passing left a void— she was the family’s storyteller, the one who taught Tomi basic Lakota phrases like “Pilamaya ye” (thank you) and “Hoka hey” (it’s a good day to die, meaning charge forward fearlessly). Grieving, Winona decided to relocate. “We need more than dust and dreams here,” she said, packing their few belongings for Queens, New York, through an urban relocation program. Tomi, clutching a beaded spider amulet from Lila, cried the whole drive, feeling like her roots were being yanked from the earth.

Urban Adaptation and Family Bonds (Ages 7-14)

Arriving in Queens at age 6 felt like stepping into a chaotic dream—towering buildings instead of open skies, honking taxis over coyote howls. Winona found work as a nurse’s aide, echoing the caregiving roles many Native women like LaDuke’s mother took in cities. They squeezed into a tiny apartment in a diverse neighborhood, surrounded by Puerto Rican and immigrant families. Tomi struggled at first: School kids teased her braids and soft rez accent, calling her “Indian princess” or worse. Like Barbara Cameron facing isolation in San Francisco, Tomi learned to navigate urban prejudice, punching a bully at 8 and earning a week’s suspension—but also a quiet respect from peers. “Don’t let ’em dim your light,” Winona advised, enrolling her in Native youth groups where she reconnected with culture through drum circles and art classes.

Home became her sanctuary. At 8, Winona gave birth to Tomi’s litt
